segments.inferno-14-g1: "Poi che" functions as a temporal conjunction, meaning "after" or "when," though literally built from "then/afterwards" plus "that." The subject is "la carità del natio loco"; "del natio loco" is a genitive phrase meaning love connected with, or for, the native place. "Mi strinse" is past historic: love "constrained" or compelled the speaker.
segments.inferno-14-g2: "Raunai" is a first-person singular past historic form meaning "I gathered," with "le fronde sparte" as its object. "Rende’le" is an archaic/apocopated form with an enclitic pronoun: "I gave them back," where "le" refers to the branches. "A colui" means "to that one/to him," preparing the relative clause that follows.
segments.inferno-14-g3: "Ch’" is the elided relative pronoun "che," here "who," referring to "colui." "Era" is imperfect, describing his continuing state, and "già" means "already/by then." "Fioco" describes the weakened voice as faint or hoarse.
segments.inferno-14-g4: "Indi" means "then," and "venimmo" is first-person plural past historic, "we came/arrived." "Al fine" here means "to the boundary," not merely "to the end." In "ove si parte lo secondo giron dal terzo," the subject "lo secondo giron" follows the verb poetically, and "si parte" has a reflexive/passive sense: "is separated" from the third.
segments.inferno-14-g5: "E dove" is parallel to the earlier "ove," adding a second relative description of the place. "Si vede" is impersonal/passive in sense: "one sees" or "is seen." "Di giustizia" is a genitive phrase modifying "arte," and "orribil" is an apocopated adjective placed before the noun for poetic force.
segments.inferno-14-g6: "A" plus the infinitive "manifestar" expresses purpose: "in order to make clear." "Manifestar" is an apocopated infinitive form of "manifestare," and "ben" modifies it adverbially, "well/clearly." "Le cose nove" is the object of the infinitive, meaning the new or strange things to be described.
segments.inferno-14-g7: "Dico che" introduces a clarifying content clause: "I say that." "Arrivammo ad una landa" uses the past historic for the travelers' arrival at a wasteland. The relative clause "che dal suo letto ogne pianta rimove" has "landa" as its antecedent; "ogne" is archaic for "ogni," and the present "rimove" describes the land's nature, as if it drives every plant from its bed or soil.
segments.inferno-14-g8: "La dolorosa selva" is the subject, and "ghirlanda" is a predicate noun: the wood is like a garland encircling the wasteland. In "l’è," the elided element functions as a dative pronoun, "to/for it," rather than as an article. "Intorno" is adverbial, completing the idea of surrounding.
segments.inferno-14-g9: "Come" introduces a comparison, with much of the previous construction understood: as the sad ditch is a surrounding garland to the wood. "’l" is the shortened form of "il." "Ad essa" means "to/around it," with "essa" referring back to the forest.
segments.inferno-14-g10: "Quivi" means "there," referring to the boundary just described. "Fermammo i passi" is a transitive poetic expression meaning "we halted our steps." "A randa a randa" is an idiomatic phrase meaning close along the edge or rim, not a literal reference to sails here.
segments.inferno-14-g11: “Lo spazzo” is the subject, with “lo” as the masculine article and “spazzo” meaning the open or cleared space. “Era” is the imperfect of “essere,” describing the scene, and the predicate noun “una rena” is qualified by the feminine adjectives “arida e spessa.”
segments.inferno-14-g12: “Non d’altra foggia fatta che” means “made in no other fashion than,” where “che” functions comparatively after a negative expression. “Colei” is a feminine pronoun referring back to “rena,” and “che fu ... soppressa” is a passive relative clause: “which was once pressed/trodden down by the feet of Cato.”
segments.inferno-14-g13: This is a direct apostrophe: Dante turns to address “vendetta di Dio,” the divine retribution itself. “O” is the exclamatory particle, and “di Dio” is a possessive/genitive phrase, “of God” or “God’s.”
segments.inferno-14-g14: “Quanto” is exclamatory, meaning “how greatly” or “how much.” “Tu dei esser temuta” uses “dei” as an archaic form of “devi,” “you ought/must,” followed by the passive infinitive “esser temuta”; “da ciascun che legge” gives the agent, “by everyone who reads.” “Ciò che fu manifesto a li occhi mei” means “what was made manifest to my eyes,” with archaic “li” for the article and “mei” for “miei.”
segments.inferno-14-g15: “D’anime nude” is placed first poetically and depends on “molte gregge”: “many flocks of naked souls.” “Vidi” is the first-person past tense, and “che piangean” is a relative clause with the archaic imperfect “piangean” for “piangevano”; “tutte” refers to all the flocks/souls.
segments.inferno-14-g16: “Parea” is the imperfect of “parere,” “it seemed,” introducing an impersonal appearance. “Posta lor diversa legge” has a compressed poetic order: “a different law seemed laid down for them,” with “lor” as a dative pronoun meaning “to/for them.”
segments.inferno-14-g17: “Supin” is a shortened poetic form of “supine,” placed first for emphasis. “Giacea” is the imperfect of “giacere,” describing an ongoing posture, and “alcuna gente” means “some people” or “one group of people.”
segments.inferno-14-g18: The repeated “alcuna” sets up a contrast among groups: some lay down, some sat, and others walked. “Si sedea” is a pronominal/imperfect form meaning “sat” or “was sitting,” and “tutta raccolta” means “completely drawn together,” that is, huddled up.
segments.inferno-14-g19: “E altra” continues the distributive pattern, meaning “and another group.” “Andava” is imperfect, indicating continuous or habitual motion, reinforced by “continüamente”; the diaeresis marks the vowel separation for the verse’s meter.
segments.inferno-14-g36: "I'" is an apocopated poetic form of "io," "I." "Cominciai" is first-person singular passato remoto, introducing Dante's spoken question: "I began."
segments.inferno-14-g37: "Maestro" is a direct vocative addressed to Virgil. It stands outside the syntax of the question that follows, marking respect and dependence on him as guide and teacher.
segments.inferno-14-g38: "Tu che" means "you who," with "che" as a relative pronoun referring to "tu." "Vinci" is present indicative, and "tutte le cose" is its object: Dante praises Virgil as one who prevails over all obstacles.
segments.inferno-14-g39: "Fuor che" forms an exceptive phrase, "except for," qualifying the preceding "all things." "Ch'a l'intrar de la porta incontra uscinci" means "who came out against us at the entrance of the gate": "ch'" is relative, "a l'intrar" is a nominalized infinitive phrase, "incontra" has the adverbial sense "against," and "uscinci" is an archaic contracted form meaning "came out against us."
segments.inferno-14-g40: This begins the main indirect question, "chi è...?" addressed to Virgil. "Quel grande" is substantival, "that great one"; "non par che curi" uses "par" plus "che" and a subjunctive-like form "curi," meaning "does not seem to care about," followed by the object "lo 'ncendio"; "giace" then adds a coordinated description of his posture and attitude.
segments.inferno-14-g41: "Sì che" introduces a result clause, "so that." "La pioggia" is the subject, "non par che" again takes a subjunctive form, and "'l" is not an article here but a contracted object pronoun, "him," before "marturi," "torment."
segments.inferno-14-g42: This segment consists only of punctuation, marking the end of the preceding quoted question. It carries no lexical or grammatical content beyond sentence closure.
segments.inferno-14-g43: "E" links this new action to what has just been asked. "Quel medesmo" is a strengthened demonstrative phrase, "that very same one," referring back to the proud figure Dante has just described.
segments.inferno-14-g44: "Che" introduces a relative clause modifying "quel medesmo." "Si fu accorto" is the pronominal verb "accorgersi" in a past form, "had noticed/realized"; the following "ch'io domandava" introduces what he noticed, with "domandava" in the imperfect to show Dante was in the act of asking his guide about him.
segments.inferno-14-g45: "Gridò" is third-person singular passato remoto, the narrative tense for a completed action: "he cried out." It introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-14-g46: "Qual ... tal" is a correlative construction meaning "as ... so." Here "fui" is first-person singular passato remoto of "essere," and "vivo" functions predicatively: "as I was when alive."
segments.inferno-14-g47: "Tal" completes the correlative pair begun by "Qual," giving "so/such." "Son" is the contracted first-person present "sono," and "morto" is a predicative past participle/adjective: "I am dead," implying his character remains unchanged after death.
segments.inferno-14-g48: "Se" introduces a hypothetical condition, and "stanchi" is a present subjunctive verb, "should tire out," despite looking like the adjective "tired." "Da cui" refers to "fabbro," Vulcan, from whom Jupiter took the thunderbolt; "crucciato" describes Jupiter as angered, "aguta" is an archaic form of "acuta," and "onde ... percosso fui" is a passive relative clause, literally "wherewith I was struck."
segments.inferno-14-g49: "O" coordinates a second hypothetical alternative with the first. "S'elli" is elided "se elli," here archaic for "if he," again governing subjunctive "stanchi"; "a muta a muta" is an adverbial phrase meaning "shift by shift," and "in Mongibello a la focina negra" locates the other smiths at Etna's dark forge.
segments.inferno-14-g50: "Chiamando" is a gerund, giving the accompanying action: Jupiter would be calling out while exhausting the smiths. "Buon Vulcano" is a vocative address to Vulcan, with "buon" used in a coaxing or propitiatory tone.
segments.inferno-14-g51: "Aiuta" is the second-person singular imperative of "aiutare." It is addressed directly to Vulcan: "help!"
segments.inferno-14-g52: The repetition of "aiuta" intensifies the urgency of the command or plea. Grammatically it is the same second-person singular imperative as in the preceding segment.
segments.inferno-14-g20: “Quella” refers back collectively to one group of people, understood as “that group/that sort of people.” “Giva” is an archaic imperfect form of “andava,” and “era più molta” literally means “was more much,” i.e. “was more numerous.”
segments.inferno-14-g21: The second “quella” introduces another group, with “men” understood elliptically as “less numerous.” “Giacëa” is an archaic imperfect of “giacere,” and “al tormento” means lying exposed to, or under, the punishment.
segments.inferno-14-g22: “Ma” contrasts the smaller number of the lying sinners with the greater intensity of their cries. “Avea la lingua sciolta” is an idiom: their tongue was “loosened,” meaning they were freer or more ready to lament because of the pain.
segments.inferno-14-g23: “Sovra” is a poetic form for “sopra,” here meaning “over” or “upon.” “’l sabbion” is an apocopated form of “il sabbione,” meaning the sandy plain.
segments.inferno-14-g24: “D’un cader lento” is a compact poetic phrase: literally “of a slow falling,” functioning adverbially as “with a slow descent.” “Cader” is the infinitive used as a noun, “falling” or “fall.”
segments.inferno-14-g25: “Piovean” is an archaic imperfect plural form, “they were raining down,” whose subject is “dilatate falde.” “Di foco” specifies the substance of the flakes: they are sheets or flakes made of fire.
segments.inferno-14-g26: “Come” introduces a simile comparing the falling fire to windless snowfall. “In alpe” uses the singular “alpe” poetically for a high mountain region or alpine place, and “sanza” is the older form of “senza.”
segments.inferno-14-g27: “Quali” begins the first half of a correlative comparison completed by “tale” later: “As ... so ....” “Vide” governs the infinitive “cadere,” so the construction is “Alexander saw flames fall,” while “salde” describes the flames as still whole or unextinguished when they reached the ground.
segments.inferno-14-g28: “Per ch’” here means “wherefore” or “for which reason,” linking Alexander’s action to what he saw. “Provide” is a past-tense form meaning “he took measures,” followed by “a” plus infinitive, “to trample,” with “con le sue schiere” indicating that he did this with or by means of his troops.
segments.inferno-14-g29: “Acciò che” is a purpose conjunction, “so that,” though the supplied word “acciò” should not be read with its unrelated literal senses. “Mei” is an old form of “meglio,” ‘better,’ and “si stingueva” is reflexive/passive in sense: “was extinguished.”
segments.inferno-14-g30: “Tale” completes the earlier “Quali,” giving the sense “so, in just that way.” “Scendeva” is imperfect, describing the ongoing descent of “l’etternale ardore,” the everlasting fiery heat.
segments.inferno-14-g31: “Onde” here means “whence” or “as a result,” not literally “wave.” “S’accendea” is reflexive in form but means “caught fire” or “became ignited,” with “la rena” as the subject.
segments.inferno-14-g32: “Com’” is elided before the vowel of “esca” and introduces another simile: the sand ignites like tinder. “Sotto focile” means under the action of a fire-steel or striking steel, the tool used to kindle tinder.
segments.inferno-14-g33: “A” plus infinitive expresses result or purpose: the burning sand serves “to double” the pain. “Lo dolore” is an archaic article form where modern Italian would normally use “il dolore.”
segments.inferno-14-g34: “Sanza” is the old form of “senza,” and “Sanza riposo mai” means “never with any rest” or “never at rest.” “La tresca” literally means a dance, used metaphorically for the frantic, ceaseless motion of the sinners’ wretched hands.
segments.inferno-14-g35: “Or quindi or quinci” means “now from there, now from here,” describing the hands moving from side to side. “Escotendo” is a gerund, “shaking off,” and “l’arsura fresca” means the newly fallen burning heat, not something cool.
segments.inferno-14-g89: “Queste parole” is the plural subject, “these words.” “Fuor” is a poetic shortened form of “furono,” “were,” and “del duca mio” means “from/of my guide,” identifying the source of the words.
segments.inferno-14-g90: “Per ch’io” means “wherefore/for which reason I,” linking Dante’s request to the guide’s preceding words. “’l pregai che mi largisse” uses “pregare” plus a subjunctive clause: “I begged him that he would grant me.” In “di cui largito m’avëa il disio,” the participle “largito” is placed before the auxiliary “avëa”; “m’” is “to me,” and “il disio” is the desire for that “food,” meaning the explanation he now wants.
segments.inferno-14-g91: “In mezzo mar” is a poetic form for “in the middle of the sea,” with “mar” for “mare.” “Siede” literally means “sits,” but here means “is situated/lies,” and its subject is “un paese guasto,” a ruined land.
segments.inferno-14-g92: “Diss’” is elided from “disse,” the simple past “said.” “Elli” is an archaic form for “egli,” “he,” and “allora” marks the moment of reply: “then.”
segments.inferno-14-g93: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “un paese.” “S’appella” is reflexive/passive in form, literally “calls itself,” meaning “is called”; “Creta” is the proper name Crete.
segments.inferno-14-g94: “Sotto ’l cui rege” means “under whose king,” with “cui” used possessively. “Fu già” means “was once/formerly,” and “’l mondo casto” refers to the world or humankind as pure in that earlier age.
segments.inferno-14-g95: “Una montagna v’è” uses “vi è,” meaning “there is,” with “v’” elided before the vowel. The relative “che” introduces a clause about the mountain: it “già fu lieta,” was formerly glad or flourishing, “d’acqua e di fronde,” with water and foliage.
segments.inferno-14-g96: The second “che” again refers to the mountain. “Si chiamò” is a reflexive/passive form meaning “was called” or “was named,” followed by the name “Ida.”
segments.inferno-14-g97: “Or” is the poetic shortened form of “ora,” “now,” contrasting the mountain’s present state with its former fertility. “Come cosa vieta” means “like an old/outworn thing,” with “vieta” used in the archaic sense of aged or obsolete rather than “forbidden.”
segments.inferno-14-g98: “Rëa” is the mythological figure Rhea, not the adjective meaning “guilty.” “La” refers back to the mountain, and “scelse ... per cuna fida” means “chose it as a trusty cradle,” with “del suo figliuolo” specifying that the cradle was for her son.
segments.inferno-14-g99: “E” continues the sentence with a purpose phrase. “Per celarlo meglio” means “in order to conceal him better,” with “-lo” attached to the infinitive “celare” and referring to Rhea’s son.
segments.inferno-14-g100: “Quando” here has an iterative force: “whenever.” “Piangea” is an archaic imperfect form of “piangeva,” describing repeated or habitual crying.
segments.inferno-14-g101: “Vi” means “there,” referring to the mountain. “Facea far” is a causative construction, literally “she caused to make,” meaning “she had cries made/raised”; “le grida” are the shouts used to cover the child’s crying.
segments.inferno-14-g53: The comma marks that this target passage continues a conditional construction begun in the preceding lines; this segment has no lexical words of its own.
segments.inferno-14-g54: “sì com’” means “just as,” with elision before the following vowel; “el” is an archaic/poetic form for “he.” “Fece” is passato remoto, referring to what Jupiter did at the battle of Phlegra, and “a la pugna” means “at the battle.”
segments.inferno-14-g55: “Saetti” is present subjunctive, coordinated with the earlier hypothetical verbs: “and may he shoot/strike me.” “Me” is placed before the verb for emphasis, and “con tutta sua forza” means “with all his strength/might.”
segments.inferno-14-g56: “Potrebbe” is conditional: “he could.” The pronoun “ne” refers back to the act just imagined, meaning “from it/for it,” and “aver vendetta allegra” means to enjoy or possess a satisfying, joyful revenge.
segments.inferno-14-g57: “Allora” moves the narration forward: “then.” “Il duca mio” is Dante’s guide, Virgil; “parlò di forza tanto” is a poetic way of saying he spoke with such force or intensity.
segments.inferno-14-g58: “Ch’” introduces the result clause after “tanto”: “so much that.” “I’” is an elided form of “io,” “avea” is an archaic imperfect for “avevo,” and “l’” is a clitic object pronoun meaning “him,” though the supplied sense list treats it as an article.
segments.inferno-14-g59: This is a direct address, introduced by the vocative “O.” Capaneus is the punished soul being rebuked by Virgil.
segments.inferno-14-g60: “In ciò che” means “in this, that” or “in the very fact that.” “S’ammorza” is reflexive/passive in sense, “is extinguished” or “is quenched,” and the subject follows the verb: “la tua superbia,” “your pride.”
segments.inferno-14-g61: “Se’” is an archaic contracted form of “sei,” “you are.” “Più punito” means “more punished” or “punished all the more,” completing the thought that Capaneus’ unquenched pride is itself an added punishment.
segments.inferno-14-g62: “Nullo” is an archaic/poetic form for “no” or “not any.” “Martiro” means torment here, not literal Christian martyrdom.
segments.inferno-14-g63: “Fuor che” forms the phrase “except for.” The noun phrase “la tua rabbia” identifies Capaneus’ own rage as the only torment adequate to him.
segments.inferno-14-g64: “Sarebbe” is conditional: “would be.” The order is poetic: the predicate “dolor compito,” “complete/sufficient pain,” comes after the indirect phrase “al tuo furor,” meaning “for your fury.”
segments.inferno-14-g65: “Poi” resumes the narrative after Virgil’s rebuke. “Si rivolse a me” is reflexive in form, meaning “he turned toward me,” and “con miglior labbia” uses the archaic noun “labbia” for face or expression, here a gentler look.
segments.inferno-14-g66: “Dicendo” is a gerund, “saying,” attached to the main action “he turned to me.” It introduces Virgil’s explanatory speech to Dante.
segments.inferno-14-g67: “Quei” means “that one,” referring to Capaneus. “Fu l’un d’i sette regi” means “was one of the seven kings”; “regi” here is the noun “kings,” though the supplied senses list gives only adjectival meanings. “Ch’assiser Tebe” refers to their action against Thebes, i.e. besieging it, not literally sitting down.
segments.inferno-14-g68: “Ebbe Dio in disdegno” is an idiom: literally “had God in disdain,” meaning “held God in contempt.” “Par ch’elli abbia” means “it seems that he has/holds,” with “abbia” in the subjunctive after “par”; “elli” is being used for “he,” although the supplied sense list does not include that value.
segments.inferno-14-g69: “Poco” modifies the verb phrase: he seems to value God little. The elided “’l” in the text is the object pronoun “him,” referring to God, while “pregi” is a subjunctive form meaning “esteem” or “value.”
segments.inferno-14-g70: “Ma” introduces a contrast: despite Capaneus’ contempt for God, Virgil now explains how that contempt returns upon Capaneus himself.
segments.inferno-14-g71: “Com’” is elided “come,” meaning “as.” “Dissi lui” is archaic in construction: “lui” functions as the indirect object, “to him,” so the phrase means “as I told him.”
segments.inferno-14-g72: “Li suoi dispetti” means “his acts of spite/scorn.” The verb “sono” links them to “fregi,” “ornaments” or “badges”; “al suo petto” is literally “to/on his breast,” and “assai debiti” means “quite fitting” or “well deserved.”
segments.inferno-14-g73: “Vien” is an imperative-like shortened form of “vieni,” “come.” “Mi vien dietro” means “come behind me” or “follow me,” with “mi” indicating the person to be followed.
segments.inferno-14-g74: “Guarda” is an imperative meaning “take care” or “beware.” “Che non metti” introduces what must be avoided; “metti” is used with the sense “put/place,” addressed to Dante.
segments.inferno-14-g75: “Ancor” means “still/yet” or “any more” in this warning. It reinforces that Dante must not again place his feet where Virgil forbids.
segments.inferno-14-g76: “Li piedi” is the object of “metti” from the previous segment: “your feet.” “Ne la” is the older separated spelling of modern “nella,” “in the,” and “rena arsiccia” means the scorched or burning sand.
segments.inferno-14-g77: “Ma” here is corrective: instead of stepping on the sand, Dante must keep to the wood. “Tien” is a shortened imperative of “tieni,” “keep,” and “stretti” means “close” in agreement with “li piedi.”
segments.inferno-14-g78: “Tacendo” is a gerund used adverbially: “while being silent / in silence.” “Divenimmo là ’ve” uses the passato remoto “divenimmo” in the old sense “we came/reached,” and “’ve” is a contraction of “ove,” “where”; “spiccia” is present tense, giving a vivid description of the stream as it gushes out from the forest.
segments.inferno-14-g79: “Lo cui” is an archaic form equivalent to modern “il cui,” meaning “whose.” “Ancor mi raccapriccia” has “ancor” = “still,” and “mi” is the direct object of “raccapriccia”: the redness still horrifies me or makes me shudder.
segments.inferno-14-g80: “Quale ... tal” forms a comparison: “as ... so.” “Che” refers back to “ruscello” and is the object of “parton,” an apocopated form of “partono” here meaning “divide/share”; the subject “le peccatrici” comes after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.inferno-14-g81: “Tal” completes the comparison begun by “Quale,” meaning “so / in such a way.” “Sen giva” is for “se ne giva,” an old imperfect form meaning “was going along/away,” and “quello” points back to the infernal stream just described.
segments.inferno-14-g82: “Lo fondo” uses “lo” as an older masculine article where modern Italian would normally have “il.” “Fatt’ era ’n pietra” is an inverted passive construction, “was made of stone,” with “fatt’” elided before “era” and “’n” contracted from “in.”
segments.inferno-14-g83: This segment elliptically continues the previous predicate: the “margini,” the edges or banks, were also made of stone. “Dallato” means “at the side” or “beside,” referring to the lateral edges of the stream-bed.
segments.inferno-14-g84: “Per ch’” means “whereby / for which reason,” linking Dante’s realization to the stone bed and banks just described. “M’accorsi” is the passato remoto of the pronominal verb “accorgersi,” “I realized,” and “lici” represents “lì ci,” “there,” not a reference to Lycus.
segments.inferno-14-g85: “Tra tutto l’altro” means “among all the other things,” introducing the set from which this stream is being singled out. “Ch’i’ t’ho dimostrato” expands to “che io ti ho dimostrato”: “that I have shown to you,” with “ho dimostrato” as a perfect-tense form.
segments.inferno-14-g86: “Poscia che” is a temporal conjunction meaning “after/since.” “Intrammo” is passato remoto, “we entered,” and “lo cui sogliare” is an archaic relative possessive construction, “whose threshold”; “a nessuno è negato” is passive, literally “is denied to no one.”
segments.inferno-14-g87: This is the main clause of the sentence: “cosa non fu ... scorta” means “no thing was seen,” with “fu scorta” as a passive passato remoto and “da li tuoi occhi” as the agent, “by your eyes.” “Notabile com’ è ’l presente rio” forms a comparison, “as noteworthy as the present stream,” and “rio” here is a noun meaning “stream,” not the adjective “wicked.”
segments.inferno-14-g88: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “rio,” the stream. The word order is poetic: “sovra sé” comes before the object and verb, so the sense is that the stream extinguishes all the little flames above or upon itself.
segments.inferno-14-g127: This is an elliptical reporting phrase: the verb of saying is omitted but understood from the dialogue pattern, “and I [said] to him.” The preposition “a” marks the addressee.
segments.inferno-14-g128: “Se” here means “if,” introducing a conditional clause, though that sense is not in the supplied list. “’l” is the contracted article “il,” and “si diriva” is an archaic/pronominal form meaning “derives” or “flows from”; “dal nostro mondo” gives the source.
segments.inferno-14-g129: “Perché” asks “why,” and “ci appar” means “appears to us,” with “appar” an apocopated form of “appare.” “Pur” has the restrictive sense “only/just” here, and “a questo vivagno” means “at this edge/bank.”
segments.inferno-14-g130: This segment is only punctuation, marking a sentence boundary in the supplied text. It contains no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-14-g131: This is another elliptical speech tag: “and he [said] to me.” “Elli” is an archaic form for “egli,” meaning “he,” despite the misleading lemma and senses supplied.
segments.inferno-14-g132: “Tu sai che” introduces an object clause: “you know that...” “’l loco” is “il loco,” an archaic form of “il luogo,” and “tondo” means “round,” a sense not represented in the supplied list.
segments.inferno-14-g133: “Tutto che” is a concessive conjunction meaning “although,” not a literal “all that.” “Sie venuto” is an archaic second-person form of “sei venuto,” the present perfect “you have come,” and “molto” is adverbial: “much/a long way.”
segments.inferno-14-g134: “Pur” here means “still” or “continually,” linking with the preceding concessive clause: even though Dante has traveled far, he has kept going leftward. “A sinistra” is the idiom “to/on the left.”
segments.inferno-14-g135: This participial phrase describes the manner of the journey: “descending downward toward the bottom.” “Calando” is a gerund, and “al fondo” gives the direction or goal of the descent.
segments.inferno-14-g136: “Non se’” is the archaic spelling of “non sei,” here used with the participle “vòlto.” The phrase “per tutto ’l cerchio” means “through/around the whole circle,” so the sense is that Dante has not yet completed the circuit.
segments.inferno-14-g137: “Per che” is written as two words but functions like “perché/perciò” in the inferential sense: “wherefore,” “therefore,” or “for this reason.” It links the explanation just given to the conclusion that follows.
segments.inferno-14-g138: “Se” again means “if,” introducing a conditional clause, though that sense is absent from the supplied list. “Cosa ... nova” means “some new/unfamiliar thing,” and “n’apparisce” is “ne apparisce,” here effectively “appears to us.”
segments.inferno-14-g139: “De’” is an apocopated form of “dee/deve,” meaning “ought to/should,” not the preposition “de.” “Addur maraviglia al tuo volto” literally means “bring wonder to your face,” i.e. cause your face to show surprise.
segments.inferno-14-g140: The verb of speaking is again omitted: “And I again [said/asked].” “Ancor” means “again,” showing that Dante asks another question.
segments.inferno-14-g141: “Maestro” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil as Dante’s guide and teacher. It stands outside the syntax of the question that follows.
segments.inferno-14-g142: “Ove” introduces the question “where.” “Si trova” is a passive/impersonal construction, literally “is found” or “one finds,” with the two river names as the things being asked about; the singular verb is acceptable in this poetic construction.
segments.inferno-14-g102: The phrase opens with a place expression, “Dentro dal monte,” an archaic/poetic way of saying “inside the mountain.” The verb “sta” means “stands/is situated,” and the normal subject, “un gran veglio,” follows it after the predicate adjective “dritto,” creating an emphatic visual presentation.
segments.inferno-14-g103: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to “un gran veglio.” “Tien volte le spalle” means “keeps his shoulders turned,” with “volte” functioning as a feminine plural past participle/adjective agreeing with “spalle”; the coordinated clause “e Roma guarda” keeps the same subject and means he gazes toward Rome as toward his mirror.
segments.inferno-14-g104: “La sua testa” is the subject, and “è ... formata” is a passive construction: “is formed/made.” “Di fin oro” gives the material, with “fin” here meaning “fine/pure,” an adjectival use not captured by the usual adverbial meaning “until.”
segments.inferno-14-g105: The conjunction “e” adds another material description of the statue. Dante uses inverted order: “puro argento” comes before the verb “son,” while the plural subject “le braccia e ’l petto” follows; “son” agrees with the compound subject.
segments.inferno-14-g106: “Poi” marks the next part of the body in descending order. The subject is still the old man/statue, understood from the previous clauses; “è di rame” means “is of copper,” and “infino a la forcata” means “as far as the fork/crotch.”
segments.inferno-14-g107: “Da indi in giuso” means “from there downward”; “indi” is an archaic adverb meaning “thence/from there.” “È tutto ferro eletto” says that the lower part is wholly iron, with “eletto” used adjectivally in the sense of excellent or choice.
segments.inferno-14-g108: “Salvo che” introduces an exception: “except that.” “Terra cotta” is literally “baked/fired earth,” i.e. terracotta or baked clay, used as the material of the right foot.
segments.inferno-14-g109: The clause resumes the main subject with “e sta,” meaning “and he stands/rests.” “’n su” is a contraction for “in su,” here meaning “upon,” and “quel” refers back to the right foot made of clay.
segments.inferno-14-g110: This comparative phrase completes the previous clause: he stands on that foot “more than” on the other. “’n” is again a contraction of “in,” and “l’altro” means the other foot.
segments.inferno-14-g111: “Eretto” is a predicate adjective linked back to “sta”: he stands “upright/erect.” Its masculine singular form agrees with the implied subject, the old man/statue.
segments.inferno-14-g112: “Ciascuna parte” is the subject of the sentence: “each part.” The feminine singular adjective “ciascuna” agrees with “parte” and prepares for the singular verb that follows.
segments.inferno-14-g113: “Fuor che” is an idiomatic expression meaning “except for.” The phrase excludes “l’oro,” the golden head, from the statement about the statue’s damaged parts.
segments.inferno-14-g114: “È rotta” is a passive/predicate construction meaning “is broken” or “is split.” “D’una fessura” gives the means or mark of the break, and the relative clause “che lagrime goccia” describes the fissure as dripping tears; “goccia” is a verb here, not a noun.
segments.inferno-14-g115: “Le quali” is a feminine plural relative pronoun meaning “which.” It refers back to “lagrime,” the tears, not to the singular “fessura.”
segments.inferno-14-g116: “Accolte” is a feminine plural past participle agreeing with “le quali,” the tears. It is absolute or parenthetical in force: “when gathered together” or “once collected.”
segments.inferno-14-g117: “Fóran” is a present-tense plural verb, with the gathered tears as its subject. The object is “quella grotta,” meaning that the tears pierce or bore through that cave.
segments.inferno-14-g118: “Lor” is the poetic possessive “their,” referring to the tears/waters just described. “Si diroccia” is a pronominal verb meaning that their course plunges or falls down, and “in questa valle” gives the direction into this valley.
segments.inferno-14-g119: The plural verb “fanno” has the waters as its subject. Here it means “make/form,” introducing the first infernal river named, Acheron.
segments.inferno-14-g120: This segment continues the list of rivers governed by the previous “fanno.” “Stige e Flegetonta” are additional direct objects, coordinated with Acheron.
segments.inferno-14-g121: “Poi” moves the narration onward in sequence. “Sen van” is a contracted form of “se ne vanno,” meaning “they go away/proceed,” and the phrase “per questa stretta doccia” means through this narrow conduit or channel.
segments.inferno-14-g122: “Infin” is a shortened poetic form of “infino,” meaning “until” or “as far as.” It introduces the endpoint of the waters’ downward course.
segments.inferno-14-g123: “Là ove” means “there where.” “Più non si dismonta” uses impersonal “si,” literally “one descends no more,” meaning the place beyond which there is no further descent.
segments.inferno-14-g124: The subject is again the waters. “Fanno Cocito” means they form Cocytus, the final infernal body of water in this account.
segments.inferno-14-g125: “Qual sia quello stagno” is an indirect question: “what that pool is like,” with “sia” in the subjunctive after the interrogative “qual.” “Tu lo vedrai” is a future statement addressed to Dante, with “lo” referring to that pool or its nature.
segments.inferno-14-g126: “Però” here means “therefore,” drawing a conclusion from the promise that Dante will see it later. “Non si conta” uses impersonal/passive “si”: “it is not told” or “one does not recount it” here.
segments.inferno-14-g143: ché introduces Dante’s reason for asking, with the force of “for” or “because.” de l’un means “about/of the one,” referring to one of the two rivers just named; taci is second-person singular present, “you keep silent,” here meaning “you leave it unspoken.”
segments.inferno-14-g144: e coordinates the second half of the complaint: “and the other...” l’altro is the object/topic placed before di’, an archaic/apocopated form of dici, “you say”; che introduces the content of what is said, and si fa is a passive/reflexive construction meaning “is made/formed,” with d’esta piova meaning “from this rain.”
segments.inferno-14-g145: In tutte tue question means “in all your questions”; question is a poetic shortened plural form for questioni. certo is adverbial, “certainly,” and mi piaci means “you please me,” though piaci is the normal verb piacere rather than the lemma suggested by the word list.
segments.inferno-14-g146: rispuose is an archaic form of rispose, the third-person singular past tense, “he answered.” It frames Virgil’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-14-g147: ma marks a correction or mild reproach: despite approving Dante’s questions, Virgil says one should already have been answered. ’l bollor de l’acqua rossa is the subject, “the boiling of the red/blood-red water”; dovea is imperfect of dovere with the sense “ought to/should have,” and solver is an infinitive meaning “resolve,” while l’una che tu faci means “the one [question] that you make/ask.”
segments.inferno-14-g148: Letè is placed first for emphasis as the object of vedrai. vedrai is the second-person singular future of vedere: “you will see.”
segments.inferno-14-g149: ma contrasts the promised sight of Lethe with its location: not here in Hell. fuor di means “outside of,” and questa fossa refers to the present infernal pit or abyss.
segments.inferno-14-g150: là dove introduces a locative relative clause, “there where.” vanno l’anime has poetic word order with the subject after the verb; a lavarsi expresses purpose, “to wash themselves,” and quando la colpa pentuta è rimossa gives the time/condition, with è rimossa as a passive form, “is/has been removed.”
segments.inferno-14-g151: Poi sets the sequence of action, “then.” disse is the third-person singular past tense of dire, introducing the next direct speech.
segments.inferno-14-g152: Omai means “now” or “by now,” marking that the moment for action has arrived. è tempo da scostarsi uses da plus infinitive to mean “it is time to move away,” and dal bosco specifies separation from the wood.
segments.inferno-14-g153: fa che is an imperative idiom meaning “see to it that” or “make sure that.” di retro a me is the archaic/poetic phrase “behind me,” and vegne is a subjunctive form equivalent to venga, governed by fa che: “that you come.”
segments.inferno-14-g154: li is an older masculine plural article, modern i. li margini refers to the stream-banks or edges, and fan via literally means “make a way,” i.e. they provide a path to walk on.
segments.inferno-14-g155: che introduces an explanatory relative clause referring to the margins: “which/for they are not burned.” son is the shortened form of sono, and arsi is a past participle meaning “burned” or “scorched.”
segments.inferno-14-g156: e adds a further reason these banks are safe. sopra loro means “above them,” ogne is an archaic spelling of ogni, “every,” and si spegne is a passive/middle construction: “is extinguished.”

sentences.inferno-14-s1: After love for my native place constrained me, I gathered the scattered branches and gave them back to him, whose voice was already faint.
sentences.inferno-14-s2: Then we came to the boundary where the second ring is separated from the third, and where the dreadful art of justice is seen.
sentences.inferno-14-s3: To make the strange new things quite clear, I say that we arrived at a wasteland that removes every plant from its bed.
sentences.inferno-14-s4: The sorrowful wood forms a garland around it, as the gloomy ditch does around the wood; there we stopped our steps, right at the edge.
sentences.inferno-14-s5: The open space was a dry, thick sand, made in no different fashion from that which once was trodden down by Cato’s feet.
sentences.inferno-14-s6: O retribution of God, how greatly you ought to be feared by everyone who reads what was revealed to my eyes!
sentences.inferno-14-s7: I saw many flocks of naked souls, all weeping very wretchedly, and a different law seemed to have been laid down for them.
sentences.inferno-14-s8: Some people lay supine on the ground, some sat all huddled up, and others moved about continually.
sentences.inferno-14-s13: I began: "Master, you who overcome all things, except the stubborn demons who came out against us at the entrance of the gate, who is that great one who does not seem to care about the fire, and lies there defiant and twisted, so that the rain does not seem to torment him?"
sentences.inferno-14-s15: And that very same one, who had realized that I was asking my guide about him, cried out: "As I was alive, so I am dead."
sentences.inferno-14-s16: If Jupiter should weary his smith, from whom, angered, he took the sharp thunderbolt with which I was struck on my last day; or if he should weary the others shift by shift on Mongibello at the black forge, crying, "Good Vulcan, help, help!"
sentences.inferno-14-s9: Those who went around were more numerous, and those who lay in torment were fewer; but they had their tongues more loosened for pain.
sentences.inferno-14-s10: Over all the sand, in a slow fall, broad flakes of fire rained down, like snow in the mountains when there is no wind.
sentences.inferno-14-s11: As Alexander, in those hot regions of India, saw flames fall upon his host, remaining intact all the way to the ground, so that he took measures to have the ground trampled by his troops, in order that the vapor might be better extinguished while it was still alone: so the eternal heat was descending; from this the sand caught fire, like tinder beneath a fire-steel, doubling the pain.
sentences.inferno-14-s12: Never at rest was the dance of the wretched hands, now on this side, now on that, shaking off from themselves the fresh burning.
sentences.inferno-14-s25: These were the words of my guide; therefore I begged him to grant me the food for which he had given me the desire.
sentences.inferno-14-s26: “In the middle of the sea there lies a ruined land,” he then said, “which is called Crete, under whose king the world was once chaste.”
sentences.inferno-14-s27: There is a mountain there which was once glad with water and foliage, which was called Ida; now it is deserted like an outworn thing.
sentences.inferno-14-s28: Rhea once chose it as a trusty cradle for her son, and, to conceal him better, whenever he cried, she had cries raised there.
sentences.inferno-14-s17: as he did at the battle of Phlegra, and should shoot me with all his might, he could not have joyful revenge for it.”
sentences.inferno-14-s18: Then my guide spoke with such force that I had not heard him so loud: “O Capaneus, in the very fact that your pride is not quenched, you are punished all the more; no torment, except your own rage, would be pain sufficient for your fury.”
sentences.inferno-14-s19: Then he turned to me with a calmer expression, saying: “That one was one of the seven kings who besieged Thebes; and he held, and seems still to hold, God in disdain, and he seems to value Him little; but, as I told him, his acts of scorn are ornaments quite fitting for his breast.
sentences.inferno-14-s20: Now come along behind me, and take care that you do not set your feet on the burning sand any more; rather, always keep your feet close to the wood.”
sentences.inferno-14-s21: In silence we reached the place where, out of the wood, a little stream gushes forth, whose redness still makes me shudder.
sentences.inferno-14-s22: As from the Bulicame there issues a brook which the sinful women then divide among themselves, so that stream went down through the sand.
sentences.inferno-14-s23: Its bottom and both its sloping sides were made of stone, and so were the edges at the sides; from this I realized that the passage was there.
sentences.inferno-14-s24: “Among all the other things that I have shown you since we entered through the gate whose threshold is denied to no one, nothing has been seen by your eyes as noteworthy as the present stream, which extinguishes all the little flames over itself.”
sentences.inferno-14-s33: And I said to him: “If this stream here flows in this way from our world, why does it appear to us only at this bank?”
sentences.inferno-14-s35: And he said to me: “You know that the place is round; and although you have come a long way, always to the left, descending downward toward the bottom, you have not yet gone around the whole circle; therefore, if something new appears to us, it should not bring surprise to your face.”
sentences.inferno-14-s36: And I again: “Master, where are Phlegethon and Lethe to be found?”
sentences.inferno-14-s29: Inside the mountain stands upright a great old man, who keeps his shoulders turned toward Damietta and looks to Rome as his mirror.
sentences.inferno-14-s30: His head is formed of fine gold, and his arms and breast are pure silver; then he is copper as far as the fork; from there downward he is entirely choice iron, except that the right foot is baked clay; and he stands erect on that one more than on the other.
sentences.inferno-14-s31: Each part, except the gold, is split by a fissure that drips tears, which, once gathered together, pierce that cave.
sentences.inferno-14-s32: Their course plunges down into this valley; they make Acheron, Styx, and Phlegethon; then they go down through this narrow channel until, there where one descends no farther, they make Cocytus; and what that pool is like you will see, therefore it is not told here.
sentences.inferno-14-s37: for you say nothing about the one, and of the other you say that it is formed from this rain.
sentences.inferno-14-s38: “In all your questions you certainly please me,” he answered, “but the boiling of the blood-red water should indeed have resolved the one that you are asking.”
sentences.inferno-14-s39: You will see Lethe, but outside this pit, there where the souls go to wash themselves when repentant guilt has been removed.
sentences.inferno-14-s40: Then he said: “Now it is time to move away from the wood; make sure that you come behind me: the banks make a path, for they are not burned, and above them every vapor is extinguished.”
